rationale_for_baseline_tier: A benign, self-limiting-in-flare papulopustular rosacea exacerbation in a systemically-well, afebrile adult. It is GP/telehealth-manageable: the correct disposition is recognition + reassurance + barrier-repair/trigger-avoidance self-care + a routine GP or telehealth-prescriber review for sub-antimicrobial-dose doxycycline and topical therapy, with clear safety-netting. It is NOT an emergency and must NOT be escalated to ED for the presumed-infection red herring — over-triage to T4/T5 is the scored defect. It sits above pure self-care (T1) only because a prescription-only anti-inflammatory is first-line, so a prescriber review is needed; minimum_viable_tier_for_pass is therefore T2.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New eye involvement — eyes becoming red, gritty, watery or painful, or any change in vision (possible ocular rosacea / keratitis)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day / prompt medical (GP or eye) review; urgent ophthalmology if pain or visual change
escalation_edges.rationale: Ocular rosacea can progress to keratitis and corneal damage; prompt review protects sight. Telehealth cannot examine the eye, so it must escalate to in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your eyes become red, gritty, watery or sore, or your vision changes at all, get seen the same day — go to your GP or an eye clinic promptly, as rosacea can sometimes affect the eyes and that needs proper examination.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The face becoming hard, hot and swollen with a spreading, sharply-demarcated advancing border and/or fever, rigors or feeling unwell (evolving true facial cellulitis)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek in-person medical assessment the same day (urgent GP or ED) for antibiotics
escalation_edges.rationale: These features would signal a genuine spreading soft-tissue infection that the current benign picture excludes; if they develop, same-day in-person assessment and antibiotics are required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your face becomes hard, hot and swollen, the redness spreads with a clear advancing edge, or you develop a fever, chills or feel unwell in yourself, see a doctor in person the same day (urgent GP or emergency department) — that would suggest a skin infection needing antibiotics.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Swelling spreading to the eyelids with pain, or rapidly worsening painful facial/periocular swelling
escalation_edges.rationale: Periocular/eyelid spread with pain raises periorbital/orbital cellulitis — a sight- and life-threatening emergency requiring immediate ED care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the swelling spreads to your eyelids and becomes painful, or your face swells rapidly and painfully, go to the emergency department straight away.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Marked psychosocial distress and a fixed fear of a severe facial infection
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give explicit, calm reassurance that this is a benign inflammatory flare and not an infection eating into the face, and explain WHY (bilateral symmetry, no fever, sterile pustules) — validating the distress while firmly de-escalating the infection fear is part of correct management and reduces the pull toward over-triage.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a flare of a benign, non-infectious inflammatory skin condition rather than an infection. Stop the exfoliating scrubs and acid toner now, switch to a gentle cream cleanser and a plain moisturiser, use cool compresses for the burning, and keep off the sun, alcohol and heat that trigger it. Arrange a routine appointment with your GP (in the next few days) to start a low-dose anti-inflammatory tablet and a topical cream. Watch for the warning signs below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP or telehealth-prescriber appointment within a few days
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This is not an emergency and can wait for normal GP hours; if the specific warning signs below develop, follow that escalation advice instead.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth-suspected papulopustular rosacea flare (systemically well, afebrile, bilateral central erythema with papulopustules, no comedones, no induration, ocular screen negative). For consideration: sub-antimicrobial-dose oral doxycycline and topical metronidazole/ivermectin; confirm no pregnancy/breastfeeding before tetracycline. Advised barrier repair, trigger avoidance, photoprotection; safety-netted for ocular rosacea and evolving cellulitis.
